text:
BILATERAL BREAST MRI WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — SURVEILLANCE

EXAMINATION: MRI of the bilateral breasts with dynamic contrast-enhanced sequences, without and with intravenous gadolinium-based contrast agent.

INDICATION: 46-year-old woman with history of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, triple-negative subtype, Stage IIB (cT2N1M0 at diagnosis), treated with neoadjuvant pembrolizumab plus dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide followed by weekly paclitaxel; subsequent left partial mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy and completion axillary lymph node dissection; adjuvant radiation therapy to the left breast and regional nodal basins; adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab maintenance (all completed). Pathologic staging ypT1cN1M0, RCB-II. All systemic and radiation therapies completed. Presenting for 18-month post-surgical surveillance breast MRI. Prior breast MRI from 12 months post-surgery available for direct comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the bilateral breasts was performed in the prone position on a dedicated breast coil at 3.0 Tesla. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1-weighted without contrast, sagittal and axial T2-weighted fat-suppressed, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, and dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) T1-weighted sequences pre-contrast and at multiple post-contrast time points following injection of 0.1 mmol/kg of gadoterate meglumine (Dotarem) intravenously. Computer-aided detection (CAD) subtraction images generated. Maximal intensity projection (MIP) reconstructions reviewed. Images reviewed in comparison with bilateral breast MRI performed at approximately 12 months post-surgery.

---

FINDINGS:

LEFT BREAST:

Breast parenchymal composition: Scattered fibroglandular tissue, mildly heterogeneous, largely consistent with prior examination. Background parenchymal enhancement is minimal bilaterally, which is favorable for lesion detection.

Surgical bed and post-treatment changes: The expected postsurgical and post-radiation changes in the left upper outer quadrant are again identified and are stable in appearance. There is a focal area of architectural distortion and linear low-T2 signal change in the left breast at approximately the 10 o'clock position, 4 cm from the nipple, corresponding to the known surgical excision site, now somewhat more organized and fibrotic compared to the 12-month post-surgery scan. A small surgical clip is visible within this region, serving as a reliable anatomical landmark. On dynamic contrast-enhanced sequences, there is a faint, thin rim of linear enhancement along the periphery of the surgical scar, exhibiting a plateau kinetic pattern; this pattern is characteristic of scar tissue and is architecturally stable compared to prior. There is no new nodular, irregular, or mass-type enhancement within or adjacent to the surgical bed. No washout kinetics are identified in this region.

Skin and chest wall: The left breast skin is mildly thickened diffusely, unchanged from prior, consistent with post-radiation sequela. There is no focal skin thickening, dimpling, or skin-associated nodular enhancement to raise concern for dermal involvement. The left pectoralis major and minor muscles are intact and uninvolved. The anterior chest wall demonstrates no abnormal signal or enhancement. No evidence of chest wall invasion or deep-margin concern.

Nipple and subareolar region: The left nipple-areolar complex appears normal in morphology and signal. No retraction, inversion, or pathologic subareolar enhancement identified. No Paget-type skin change.

Additional lesions: No new mass, non-mass enhancement, or focus suspicious for malignancy is identified within the remainder of the left breast parenchyma. There are no new satellite nodules, no intramammary lymph nodes with abnormal morphology, and no areas of restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC mapping that would raise concern for viable tumor.

LEFT AXILLA AND REGIONAL NODES:

Left axillary surgical bed: Post-surgical changes are present in the left axilla consistent with prior lymph node dissection, including expected fibrotic stranding and absence of the normal axillary fat pad architecture in the lower axillary region. These changes are stable and expected. No new or enlarging lymph nodes are identified in the axillary level I, II, or III regions. No rounded, hypo-T2, or abnormally enhancing nodal structures are seen. The level I-III axillary soft tissues appear stable and post-operative in character.

Supraclavicular region: No pathologically enlarged or suspicious supraclavicular lymph nodes identified on survey of these regions. One small, bean-shaped, fatty-hilum-bearing lymph node is seen in the right supraclavicular region, measuring approximately 6 mm; this is unchanged from prior and morphologically benign.

Internal mammary chain: There is no abnormal enhancement or adenopathy in the bilateral internal mammary chains.

RIGHT BREAST:

Breast parenchymal composition: Scattered fibroglandular tissue, similar to the contralateral side. Background parenchymal enhancement is minimal.

Mass lesions: No suspicious mass identified. There are no areas of irregular, spiculated, or rim-enhancing lesions. No satellite nodules.

Non-mass enhancement: No suspicious non-mass enhancement. There is a small region of linear enhancement in the right upper outer quadrant following a ductal distribution, which was also present on the prior 12-month surveillance examination and is interpreted as benign-appearing ductal enhancement, stable and without progressive features. No biopsy is indicated.

Other findings: No skin thickening, nipple retraction, or axillary adenopathy on the right. Right axilla: several small, oval, fatty hilum-bearing lymph nodes identified, the largest measuring approximately 8 mm in short axis, morphologically benign and stable.

DIFFUSION-WEIGHTED IMAGING:

DWI sequences evaluated at b=0, b=800, and b=1000 s/mm2 with corresponding ADC maps. No areas of restricted diffusion suspicious for residual or recurrent malignancy identified in either breast or the axillary/nodal regions. The surgical scar in the left breast demonstrates expected T2 dark fibrosis without restricted diffusion.

KINETIC ANALYSIS (CAD):

Computer-aided detection subtraction images reviewed. No color-coded washout kinetics identified in the left breast surgical bed or elsewhere. The thin peripheral scar enhancement in the left breast demonstrates plateau kinetics (type II curve), stable from prior. No type III washout curves identified in either breast.

COMPARISON WITH PRIOR STUDY:

Direct comparison with bilateral breast MRI from approximately 12 months post-surgery demonstrates no interval change of any significance. The post-operative and post-radiation changes in the left breast are stable and maturing. No new enhancing lesions, no interval development of mass or non-mass enhancement in either breast, and no new axillary or regional adenopathy. The previously described faint peripheral scar enhancement is slightly less conspicuous on the current examination, in keeping with continued scar maturation.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Left breast: Stable post-operative and post-radiation changes at the prior lumpectomy bed in the left upper outer quadrant. The surgical scar shows the expected architectural distortion and fibrosis with thin, stable peripheral scar enhancement exhibiting plateau kinetics — no features to suggest residual or recurrent malignancy. The surgical clip is in unchanged position.

2. No new suspicious enhancement, mass, or non-mass enhancement identified in the left breast. No restricted diffusion on DWI.

3. Left axilla: Stable post-lymphadenectomy changes. No new or suspicious adenopathy at axillary level I, II, or III. No suspicious supraclavicular or internal mammary nodal disease identified.

4. Right breast: Unchanged, no suspicious finding. The small area of ductal enhancement in the right upper outer quadrant is stable and interpreted as benign.

5. Overall, no imaging evidence of local recurrence or contralateral breast malignancy at this 18-month post-surgical surveillance examination. Findings are entirely consistent with the expected appearance of a treated and quiescent surgical bed without imaging features of disease recurrence.

Electronically signed by: Attending Breast Radiologist
Department of Breast Imaging, Radiology
